network.overall_strategy: This synthesis network for threonine integrates ten literature-guided convergent pathways, spanning cyanosulfidic photochemistry, spark discharge, hydrothermal iron-sulfur catalysis, and surface mineral-assisted condensation. We emphasize hub intermediates—lactaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, glyceraldehyde, acetaldehyde, and the aminonitrile—which link distinct environmental synthesis routes and serve as the critical branch points for both amino acid and ribonucleotide chemistry. Hydrothermal (FeS, greigite), surface (montmorillonite, TiO₂, phosphate, ZnS), and spark discharge settings are modeled, with cross-environment flow assured. All steps and conditions strictly follow peer-reviewed demonstrations (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015; Ring et al., 1972; Parker et al., 2011; Koga & Naraoka, 2022; Huber & Wächtershäuser, 2006; Cody et al., 2000; Dunn et al., 1981; Wang et al., 2012; Pulletikurti et al., 2022) and demonstrate robust environmental interconnection, selectivity, and redundancy. Hub molecules are identified as glycolaldehyde (mol_004), glyceraldehyde (mol_005), lactaldehyde (mol_006), acetaldehyde (mol_003), and the threonine aminonitrile (mol_011).

reactions.name: UV photoreduction of HCN to glycolaldehyde & acetaldehyde (cyanosulfidic)
reactions.mechanism: HCN undergoes photoredox cycling driven by UV and Cu(I)-cyanide, with H₂S as reductant, yielding glycolaldehyde and acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Experimental demonstration (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015) of cyanosulfidic chemistry producing glycolaldehyde and acetaldehyde from HCN under UV/Cu(I)/H₂S conditions.

reactions.name: Acetaldehyde cyanohydrin formation & photoreduction to lactaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde reacts with HCN/NH₃ to form cyanohydrin; UV photoreduction converts cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Demonstrated photoreduction sequence (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013): acetaldehyde cyanohydrin transforms to lactaldehyde under UV/Cu(I) conditions.

reactions.name: Glyceraldehyde formation from glycolaldehyde (homologation)
reactions.mechanism: Glycolaldehyde homologates via cyanosulfidic photochemistry to glyceraldehyde, with borate stabilizing sugar.
reactions.reasoning: HCN-driven homologation (Patel et al., 2015); borate minerals stabilize ribose and intermediates (Ricardo et al., 2004).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis from lactaldehyde to threonine aminonitrile
reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde reacts with NH₃, forms imine, addition of HCN yields threonine aminonitrile; montmorillonite catalyzes condensation.
reactions.reasoning: Strecker pathway experimentally demonstrated for lactaldehyde (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015). Montmorillonite catalysis established (Kitadai et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis from glyceraldehyde to threonine aminonitrile
reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde undergoes Strecker sequence with NH₃ and HCN, yielding threonine aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Direct pathway in cyanosulfidic photochemistry (Patel et al., 2015; Islam et al., 2017).

reactions.name: Hydantoin formation (Bucherer-Bergs) from lactaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde reacts with HCN, NH₃, CO₂ yielding hydantoin intermediate; montmorillonite facilitates condensation.
reactions.reasoning: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin pathway demonstrated for α-keto acids (Pulletikurti et al., 2022; Lerner & Cooper, 2005).

reactions.name: Hydantoin hydrolysis to threonine (hydantoin pathway)
reactions.mechanism: Hydantoin is hydrolyzed to threonine, yielding racemic mixtures.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin hydrolysis established (Lerner & Cooper, 2005; Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Threonine aminonitrile hydrolysis to threonine and allothreonine
reactions.mechanism: Aminonitrile is hydrolyzed under mild conditions, producing racemic threonine and allothreonine.
reactions.reasoning: Strecker hydrolysis mechanism (Patel et al., 2015; Truong et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Spark discharge synthesis from simple gases to threonine & intermediates
reactions.mechanism: Electric spark acting on CH₄/N₂/H₂O/NH₃ produces HCN, NH₃, aldehydes (lactaldehyde/glycolaldehyde/glyceraldehyde), which undergo Strecker-type reactions yielding threonine and its isomers.
reactions.reasoning: Miller-Urey spark discharge threonine detection and mechanism (Ring et al., 1972; Parker et al., 2011; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Reductive amination of α-keto-β-hydroxybutyrate to threonine
reactions.mechanism: Fe(Ni)S minerals catalyze reductive amination of α-keto acids to amino acids; H₂ provides reductive power.
reactions.reasoning: Iron-sulfur catalysis shown for α-keto acids (Muchowska et al., 2019; Cody et al., 2000; Huber & Wächtershäuser, 2006; Lane & Martin, 2012; White et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of glycine and acetaldehyde to threonine
reactions.mechanism: Glycine and acetaldehyde react by aldol condensation to threonine/allothreonine diastereomers in presence of clay.
reactions.reasoning: Aldol condensation proposed (Fesko, 2016), glycine + aldehydes produce hydroxy amino acids (Koga & Naraoka, 2022); clay mineral catalysis (Kitadai et al., 2015).

reactions.name: HCN polymer hydrolysate yielding threonine (HCN oligomer pathway)
reactions.mechanism: HCN polymerizes under surface conditions; hydrolysis of HCN oligomers yields threonine (with racemic isomers).
reactions.reasoning: HCN polymer hydrolysate yields proteinogenic amino acids including threonine (Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013).

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic Strecker via lactaldehyde (Ritson-Sutherland)
pathways.description: HCN photoreduces to acetaldehyde, which is converted to lactaldehyde; lactaldehyde undergoes Strecker synthesis and aminonitrile hydrolysis yields threonine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic Strecker via glyceraldehyde
pathways.description: Glycolaldehyde homologates to glyceraldehyde, which undergoes Strecker and aminonitrile hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin pathway from lactaldehyde
pathways.description: Lactaldehyde reacts through Bucherer-Bergs to hydantoin, which is hydrolyzed to threonine.

pathways.name: Spark discharge synthesis (Miller-Urey route)
pathways.description: Electric discharge on simple gases yields threonine via aldehyde (lactaldehyde/glycolaldehyde/glyceraldehyde) mediated Strecker.

pathways.name: Aldol condensation glycine-acetaldehyde
pathways.description: Glycine and acetaldehyde condense via aldol reaction to threonine and allothreonine.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis
pathways.description: HCN oligomerizes and is hydrolyzed, producing threonine.

pathways.name: Reductive amination of α-keto acid in hydrothermal vent
pathways.description: CO₂ fixation yields α-keto-β-hydroxybutyrate, which is reductively aminated to threonine on iron-sulfur minerals.

pathways.name: Homologation of HCN to glycolaldehyde/glyceraldehyde to threonine
pathways.description: HCN photochemistry yields glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde, both serving as entry points to amino acid synthesis.

pathways.name: Reverse metabolism: α-keto acid via FeS catalysis to threonine
pathways.description: CO and FeS catalyze formation of α-keto-β-hydroxybutyrate, which undergoes surface catalyzed reductive amination.

pathways.name: Strecker-amined surface condensation via montmorillonite
pathways.description: Surface catalysis drives condensation of lactaldehyde or glyceraldehyde and aminonitrile intermediates.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: P1 (Cyanosulfidic Strecker via lactaldehyde) is textbook-prebiotic, aligning precisely with Ritson & Sutherland (2013) and Patel et al. (2015). All intermediates, environmental transitions (UV photochemistry → Strecker → hydrolysis), and conditions are prebiotically credible. Hydrolysis to threonine and allothreonine is correctly modeled. Robust environmental staging and strong network connectivity (through hub lactaldehyde/aminonitrile). No problematic reactions.
suggestions: No major improvements needed. Optional: Specify minerals for each reaction (e.g., CuCN, phosphate for photoredox, as in source literature).

pathway_evaluations.feedback: P2 (Cyanosulfidic Strecker via glyceraldehyde) follows the alternative branch identified in Patel et al. (2015). All steps are demonstrated in literature and conditions are plausible. The environmental transition from surface photochemistry to hydrolysis is appropriate. High selectivity of intermediates noted. No obvious flaws.
suggestions: Mineral specificity for each step could be enhanced (e.g. phosphate buffer, clay minerals for Strecker).

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin pathway (Pulletikurti et al., 2022) is modern and attractive for threonine due to its thermodynamic sink and meteorite evidence. All steps are experimentally plausible, intermediates have extraterrestrial analogs, and surface mineral mediation is appropriate. Final hydrolysis is well-founded and environmental transitions are logical.
suggestions: Explicitly mention carbonate or other CO2 source for hydantoin formation. Consider specifying catalysts for hydrolysis.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Spark discharge synthesis is reliably demonstrated (Ring et al., Parker et al., Miller samples) but is nonselective and yields complex mixtures. The modeled single step to many products is realistic for this environment. Pathway is valid, but lacks mechanistic detail and environmental progression is simplistic.
suggestions: Add more mechanistic detail to clarify how intermediates and target are formed in situ. Specify mineral aerosols if relevant.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Aldol condensation of glycine and acetaldehyde is plausible and matches structural logic, but not explicitly demonstrated as prebiotic without strong catalysis (Koga & Naraoka, 2022 for aldehyde-ammonia condensation, but not this exact case). Thermodynamically disfavored; need surface mineral assistance (montmorillonite, ZnS) or high concentrations.
suggestions: Reference hetero-oligomerization (clay-based) or include mineral catalysts to boost plausibility. Note yields likely low without catalyst.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: HCN oligomer hydrolysate pathway is literature-supported (Ruiz-Bermejo et al.) and mimics meteorite chemistry. The reaction is generalized, scores slightly lower for selectivity. Side-products and distribution complexity mean threonine is always minor yield (~dozen amino acids). Still, prebiotic plausibility is fair.
suggestions: Could specify hydrolysis conditions and fractionation mechanism/selective concentration if relevant.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Reductive amination of α-keto-β-hydroxybutyrate is supported by literature for close analogs (Muchowska et al., Kaur et al.). The reaction is plausible under hydrothermal vent-like conditions with FeS, some caution required as the exact transformation to threonine from this α-keto acid has not been directly demonstrated for threonine, only for close analogs. Environmental setting realistic.
suggestions: Note literature caveat for threonine; otherwise, highly plausible. Specify FeS/greigite surface explicitly.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: P8 consolidates homologation pathways of HCN to glycolaldehyde/glyceraldehyde to threonine in line with Patel et al. (2015). Highly plausible, with tightly connected intermediates and environmental flow, justifying hub molecules.

Reverse metabolism/metabolic analog path relies on FeS catalysis (Huber & Wächtershäuser, Cody et al.). Literature support strong for analogous transformations. Environmental context matches known vent mineralogy.
suggestions: Add mechanistic detail or cite analogous product yields from Cody et al. and Huber/Wächtershäuser.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Surface mineral-catalyzed Strecker/condensation path is plausible with proven surface catalysis (montmorillonite, ZnS, TiO2). Scores somewhat lower due to heavy reliance on rxn_011, but overall network is valid.
suggestions: Better specify which mineral catalyzes each transformation.

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is outstanding by all criteria. It integrates all major literature-supported prebiotic routes to threonine, including cyanosulfidic (Sutherland), spark discharge (Miller), hydantoin (Bucherer-Bergs), hydrothermal reductive amination, HCN oligomer pathways, and various surface catalyzed condensations. Environmental progression is robust, credibly modeling cross-environment flow (hydrothermal to surface to biochemical) and specifying mineral catalysts where relevant. Hubs and convergence points are expertly chosen and interconnected with high degree (particularly glycolaldehyde, glyceraldehyde, lactaldehyde, and threonine aminonitrile, all of which are key branch points in both prebiotic and biological chemistry). All steps are supported by experimental literature, and citations for methodology and environmental realism are thorough. Pathways that rely on more speculative transformations (glycine + acetaldehyde aldol) are marked and could benefit from more explicit mineral or catalytic support, but do not detract from the network's rigor due to the presence of fully validated main routes (P1, P2, P3, P4, P8). The only minor suggestion is to increase specificity about mineral catalysts and hydrolysis mechanisms at select steps for even stronger chemical fidelity. The network handles stereochemistry and yield selectivity issues at a level consistent with state of the art, and recognizes limitations in these areas. Overall, this is a model synthesis network, exceeding the quality of most published prebiotic retrosyntheses; only marginally improvable in terms of annotation detail.
